coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots integrated into nickel-cobalt hydroxide as superior electrode for supercapattery
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Yangbo
Han, Chong
Si, Huizheng
Liu, Kaiyu
Liu, Hongtao
Sang, Shangbin
Wu, Qiumei
coredata.dc:description: Nickel hydroxide (Ni(OH)2) is one of the most valuable electrode materials for supercapacitors. Owing to the low stability and conductivity of Ni(OH)2 electrode, the preparation of a fast and high-stability Ni(OH)2 electrode is a significant challenge. Nickel hydroxide integrated with cobalt and carbon dots (CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2) as a high performance electrode has been prepared by cathodic electrodeposition for supercapattery in this work. Compared with unintegrated Ni(OH)2 electrode or carbon dots integrated Ni(OH)2 electrode, the introduction of Co greatly improves the stability of the Ni(OH)2 electrode, particularly at high current density. The as-obtained CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode presents a high specific capacitance of 338.6 mAh/g at 5 A/g and an extraordinary cycling stability with 1000 cycles even under a high current density of 40 A/g (retaining 84.6%). Besides, the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode exhibits an energy density of 281.2 Wh/kg at a power density of 57.2 kW/kg. Such a glorious stable CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode may provide significant insight into future multifarious electronics and electrified vehicle applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nickel hydroxide
Cathodic electrodeposition
Cobalt
Carbon dots
Supercapattery

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 thin-film electrode prepared by cathode electrodeposition.
SEM images of 2.4 mg/cm2 (a) Ni(OH)2 electrode, (b) CDs-Ni(OH)2 electrode, (c-d) CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode.
(a-b) High-resolution image, (c-h) TEM-mapping image of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode, (i-k) XPS image of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode.

CV curves of (a) Ni(OH)2 electrode, CDs-Ni(OH)2 electrode and CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode at 15 mV/s, (b) CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode at different sweep velocities, (c) Charge-discharge curves of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode under different loads, (d) Schematic diagram of load and specific capacity of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode and contrast Ni(OH)2 electrode, (e) charge curve under different load, (f) discharge curve.

(a) charge-discharge curve of 2.4 mg/cm2 CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode at different current densities, (b) EIS impedance curves of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2, CDs-Ni(OH)2, Ni(OH)2 electrode (c) percentage curve of 1000 charge-discharge specific capacity of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2, CDs-Ni(OH)2, Ni(OH)2 electrode.

Cyclic volt-ampere curves of Ni/Zn supercapattery at (a) Cyclic voltammetry curve, (b) charge-discharge curve, (c) different sweep speeds, (d) charge-discharge curves at different current densities, (e) specific capacity changing curve with current density, (f) Ragone curve of Ni/Zn supercapattery, and (g) specific capacity retention rate at 2000 cycles at 40 A/g.
Electrochemical performance comparison between some electrodes.

row.entry: 81.6% capacity retention after cycling at 10 A/g for 1000 cycles
74% capacity retention after cycling at 20 A/g for 1000 cycles
80.8% capacity retention after cycling at 10 A/g for 2000 cycles
82.2% capacity retention after cycling at 10 A/g for 1000 cycles
80.2% capacity retention after cycling at 10A/g for 5000 cycles
84.6% capacity retention after cycling at 40 A/g for 1000 cycles

head.ce:title: Carbon dots integrated into nickel-cobalt hydroxide as superior electrode for supercapattery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Power Sources, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Power Sources, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aHunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Power Sources, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key of Powder Metallurgy Laboratory, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key of Powder Metallurgy Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bState Key of Powder Metallurgy Laboratory, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nickel hydroxide (Ni(OH)2) is one of the most valuable electrode materials for supercapacitors. Owing to the low stability and conductivity of Ni(OH)2 electrode, the preparation of a fast and high-stability Ni(OH)2 electrode is a significant challenge. Nickel hydroxide integrated with cobalt and carbon dots (CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2) as a high performance electrode has been prepared by cathodic electrodeposition for supercapattery in this work. Compared with unintegrated Ni(OH)2 electrode or carbon dots integrated Ni(OH)2 electrode, the introduction of Co greatly improves the stability of the Ni(OH)2 electrode, particularly at high current density. The as-obtained CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode presents a high specific capacitance of 338.6 mAh/g at 5 A/g and an extraordinary cycling stability with 1000 cycles even under a high current density of 40 A/g (retaining 84.6%). Besides, the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode exhibits an energy density of 281.2 Wh/kg at a power density of 57.2 kW/kg. Such a glorious stable CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode may provide significant insight into future multifarious electronics and electrified vehicle applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In today's rapidly advancing technology, the increasing dependence on electrical energy has made the urgent need to improve the performance of energy storage devices more urgent than ever [1,2]. All of today's research and development efforts are aimed at pushing supercapacitor and battery performance to the upper right part of the Ragone diagram [3–5]. This highlights a trend that the line between supercapacitors, more specifically those based on redox pseudocapacitive materials, and high-rate batteries are becoming increasingly blurred [6,7]. Supercapattery with both high power density and high energy density are becoming the future direction of electrochemical energy storage research [8,9]. pseudocapacitor, as a kind of supercapacitor, have the advantage of higher specific capacity compared with electric double-layer capacitor, but their cycle stability has always been a problem that limits their applications [10,11]. At the same time, the electrode materials of pseudocapacitance capacitors put into use today are relatively high expenses, and the development of low-cost and high-stability pseudocapacitor may be the research direction of new supercapacitor [12,13]. Since nickel hydroxide is characterized by abundant resources, high theoretical capacity and low cost, it is considered to be an excellent pseudo-capacitor material, but its electrical conductivity and stability have been limiting performance issues, especially at high current density [14,15]. In previous studies, the carbon dots (CDs) were integrated into electrodes, which effectively inhibited the agglomeration of nickel hydroxide crystals during the electrodeposition process, and kept the nano-sized crystal grains at a lower dimension, which made the electrode has excellent electrochemical performance [16]. However, there are still many problems with the current electrode distance reaching the ideal state that can be applied. For example, the load still needs to be further improved, especially since the stability problem is still difficult to solve [17–19].

As an element adjacent to nickel in the periodic table, cobalt has similar chemical properties to nickel. And the physical and chemical properties of its hydroxides, especially the oxidation–reduction behavior, are similar to those of nickel hydroxide used as supercapacitor electrode material [20–23]. Although the theoretical capacity of cobalt hydroxide is slightly inferior to that of nickel hydroxide, its stability has always been a commendable place [24,25]. The introduction of cobalt into nickel-related compounds has always been a way to stabilize the crystal lattice and improve the cycle life of the material, such as lithium cobalt oxide and nickel cobalt oxide in the positive electrode of lithium battery. If we introduce the Co element into the nickel hydroxide thin film electrode in the form of hydroxide, the problem of poor stability may be solved, and further higher loading electrodes will also be able to be synthesized. Currently, nickel-cobalt double hydroxide is widely investigated as a positive electrode material of supercapacitor. In 2017, Tuyen et al. [26]. electrodeposited a continuous layer of Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 on a stainless steel current collector. Preparation of charge storage electrodes with a high specific capacity, and 2020 Atefeh et al. [27]. successfully synthesized Co(OH)2@Ni(OH)2 sheet on nickel wire (NW) coated with 3D-Ni film through electrochemical methods. These studies have confirmed the feasibility of our ideas, but whether the introduction of cobalt can make the film electrode have a longer service life under high load and high rate, whether the nickel hydroxide nanocrystalline grains can still maintain the near-critical size still requires practical experiments to research [28–31].

In this work, we have successfully synthesized a highperformance CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode for supercapattery by cathodic electrodeposition. Results show that the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode delivers a high specific capacitance and an excellent capacitance retention.

Besides, a Ni/Zn supercapattery assembled with a zinc flake as negative electrode and a CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode as positive electrode exhibits high power density, high energy density and remarkable cycling stability, demonstrating its excellent application prospects, especially for high-current energy storage devices.

All the chemicals were analytically pure, Nickel nitrate hexahydrate and cobalt nitrate hexahydrate were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The titanium mesh (TM) was purchased from Anping Shengzhuo stainless steel mesh factory.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ni(OH)2 thin film electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode were prepared by constant current cathodic electrodeposition with titanium mesh (TM) as a cathode and a 2 cm × 2 cm Pt sheet as an anode. (See Fig S1, supplementary data) The preparation of carbon dots (CDs) can refer to the previous studies published by our group [32]. 50 mL solution containing 0.1 mol/L M(NO3)2 and 0.1 g/L CDs was used as the initial electrolyte solution (M=Ni Co,[Ni2+]+[Co2+]=0.1 M). The deposition current was set at 25 mA/cm2. The CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 thin-film electrode with various loadings were prepared through different deposition times such as 100 s, 150 s, 200 s, 300 s, 400 s. What's more, the ratio of nickel to cobalt in thin film electrodes was controlled by changing the content of cobalt nitrate in an electrolyte such as 7%, 9%, 11% and 13%.

The microstructure and element distribution of the samples were observed by FE-SEM (Nova NanoSEM 230) and EDS mapping. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Jem-2100f) was used to characterize the crystal structure of the studied samples. Elemental analysis of the sample was analyzed by Inductive Coupled Plasma Emission Spectrometer (ICP, TCP-5100-VDV) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, K-Alpha1063). The binding energy of the spectra was corrected with C 1s (284.8 eV). Functional groups of samples were identified by FT-IR spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher, iS20).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), Charge-discharge (CD), and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were measured by the electrochemical workstation (DH 7000, Donghua Analysis) in a traditional three-electrode system. The Pt electrode was used as the auxiliary electrode. The reference electrode was Hg/HgO (1 M KOH) electrode.

Fig. (2a-c) are SEM images of Ni(OH)2 electrode, CDs-Ni(OH)2 electrode and CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode with loadings of 2.4 mg/cm2, respectively. Compared to the Ni(OH)2 electrode, CDs-Ni(OH)2 electrode exhibits a porous, three-dimensional structure with stacked spherical particles, CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode is more dense and uniform. The results of the further magnification of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode in Fig. (2d) show that, the compact surface shown by CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 is stacked by more particles with a smaller radius, which means the construction of materials with larger specific surface area and more pores, this structure will also provide more ion transport channels to further improve the conductivity of the electrode.

Fig. 3 (a-h) shows the TEM image of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode at the loading of 7.2 mg/cm2. It can be observed that the lattice fringes of Ni(OH)2, Co(OH)2,and CDs are staggered in Fig. 3(a), the three nanocrystalline grains agglomerate and stack with each other during the electrodeposition process, and finally form the macroscopic structure and morphology of the material. Moreover, the Ni(OH)2 and Co(OH)2 lattice fringes can be seen in the figure. Compared with the standard cards PDF#38-0715 and PDF#45-0031 (See Fig. S4, supplementary data), the lattice spacing of 0.26 nm in Fig. 3(b) conforms to the Ni(OH)2 (0 1 2) crystal plane, and the lattice spacing of 0.23 nm conforms to the Co(OH)2 (0 0 2) crystal plane. At the same time, the nano-grain size of Ni(OH)2 remains below 10 nm, which means that the introduction of the Co element does not destroy the inhibitory effect of the integrating of CDs on the original grain size of nickel hydroxide, and it can be expected nickel hydroxide maintained this size should still have excellent electrochemical performance.

At the same time, we found that the integrating of CDs in the previous study makes the electrode looser than the unintegrated sample, and the introduction of Co makes the electrode more uniform and dense. Under certain viewing angles, the two seem to be contradictory. However, after an in-depth investigation, it is found that the nano-grain size of the prepared nickel hydroxide thin film electrode is always kept at a lower level compared to the blank sample. This is the decisive factor that affects the performance of the electrode, especially under conditions of high load and stability. This is also consistent with the results of our initial investigation of the effect of current density on the deposition of nickel hydroxide thin film electrodes. The three-dimensional nano-flower-like loose porous structure formed by low current deposition is far inferior to the electrode performance of the disk-shaped crystal grain accumulation structure obtained by high current deposition. Keeping the nickel hydroxide nanocrystals in a near-critical size (5–10 nm) with excellent conductivity is the optimal solution for the film electrode to have high performance at the same time under high loading.

The TEM-mapping image of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode is also shown in Fig. 3(c-h). The Ni, Co, C, and O elements can be observed from the figure, and the rough morphology can be observed in the mapping image of each element. This once again strongly proves the successful introduction of cobalt element and the successful integrating of carbon dots, and also proves that nickel hydroxide, cobalt hydroxide, and carbon dots are combined on the nanometer scale electrode.

The chemical composition of the as-obtained electrode was further investigated by XPS analysis.The full-scan spectra indicate that the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode contains elements of Ni, Co, and C (See Fig. S5, supplementary data). The high-resolution XPS spectra of specific core-level transitions is shown in Fig. 3(i-k).
Fig. 3(i) shows the C 1s spectra of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode, the curve can be discovered one peaks at 284.8 eV as C 1s.

Fig. 3(j) shows the Ni 2p spectra of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode.The intense features at 855.78 eV and 873.54 eV are assigned to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 of Ni2+, respectively. The spin-energy separation of 17.76 eV indicates the existence of Ni(OH)2 in CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode. Other two peaks located at 861.96 and 879.94 eV are the corresponding satellite peaks.

Fig. 3(k) shows the Co 2p spectra of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode, which can be divided into four peaks, the two fitting peaks at 782.07 eV and 797.43 eV can be identified as Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 signals of Co2+, indicating the existence of Co(OH)2 in CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode. The remaining two peaks located at 785.69 and 803.53 eV are satellite peaks.

Furthermore, the XPS result shows that the Ni-Co molar ratio in the electrode (88:12) is similar to that in the electrolyte (91:9). The sample was analyzed by IR spectra (See Fig. S6, supplementary data). There is a clear absorption peak at 534 cm−1, 640 cm−1,indicating the presence of Co-OH, Ni-OH [38–40]. The XPS and IR spectra results demonstrate the successful synthesis of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode.

To study the influence of Co element content on the performance of electrodes, we will use the electrochemical deposition method to prepare electrodes with different Ni/Co ratios, and CV analysis, constant current charge and discharge analysis and EIS analysis were studied in 1 M KOH solution. EIS analysis was conducted with the frequency ranges from 0.01 Hz to 1,000,000 Hz and AC perturbation of 10 mV. All tests are carried out at 25 °C.

In summary, we have determined that when the Co element is introduced into Ni(OH)2, the element ratio of Co: Ni in the deposition electrolyte is maintained at 9:91. The CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode was prepared based on this, the prepared deposition current was 25 mA/cm2, and the electrode loading was 1.2, 2.4, 4.8, 7.2, 9.6 mg/cm2, respectively.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the CV curve of the Ni(OH)2 electrode, CDs-Ni(OH)2 electrode and the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode at 15 mV/s and a voltage range of 0–1.0 V. It can be observed from the figure that the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode has higher redox peak current compared with the contrast electrode, which means that CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 sample has higher electrochemical activity. Fig. 4(b) is the CV image of the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode at different scanning speeds. From the figure, it can be observed that when the scanning speed gradually increases from slow to fast, the shape of the CV image remains constant. This means that the electrode still has stable electrochemical performance under the condition of charging and discharging with high current and high speed.

To study the electrochemical performance of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode under high loading, we prepared the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrodes of 1.2, 2.4, 4.8, 7.2, 9.6 mg/cm2 loading under the deposition current of 25 mA/cm2, the Ni(OH)2 electrode and CDs-Ni(OH)2 electrode with the same loading capacity were prepared for comparison. The constant current charge and discharge test was carried out at a current density of 5 A/g. The result is shown in Fig. 4(c-f).

Fig. 4(c) shows the 5 A/g constant current charge-discharge curves of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrodes under different loading. It can be seen from the figure that as the load increases, the discharge time of the electrode gradually decreases, and the specific capacity gradually decreases. Fig. 4(d) shows the specific capacity images of the Ni(OH)2 electrode, CDs-Ni(OH)2 electrode and CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode at 5 A/g under different loading, the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode display a better specific capacity compared with the contrast electrodes. In addition, the specific capacity of the contrast sample only has the data before the loading of 4.8 mg/cm2. This is because when the loading is further increased, the stability of the contrast sample no longer supports the normal charge and discharge test. During the charge and discharge process, it can be observed that the active material continuously falls off the substrate of the contrast electrode, while the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode maintain in a stable state. This favorably demonstrates the breakthrough in the stability of the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode under high loading. As can be observed in Fig. 4(e), the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode has a lower charging plateau implying better conductivity. From Fig. 4(f), it can be concluded that the Co incorporation does not significantly reduce the discharge plateau, which also proves the correctness of the results of the above investigation on the Co content in the electrolyte.

All in all, the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode exhibits the specific capacity of 338.6 mAh/g, 289.1 mAh/g, 216.7 mAh/g at 1.2 mg/cm2, 2.4 mg/cm2, 4.8 mg/cm2, loading, respectively. Even if the amount increased to 9.6 mg/cm2, the specific capacity still retains 133.2 mAh/g, which means that the electrode with excellent electrochemical capacity at high loadings has been synthesized. Furthermore, the higher specific capacity of the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode may be attributed to the even and dense stacking of the particles with a small radius on its surface, and the expansion of the surface area has provided more active sites and ion transport channels, which not only makes it smooth of the transmission of electrolyte ions but also allows more active sites to be contacted during the process of charging or discharging so that the conductivity of the electrode can be further improved.

To further investigate the stability of the electrode, rate capability study was conducted with a loading of 2.4 mg/cm2 at a current densities of 5, 10, 15 and 20 A/g, respectively. For comparison, the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode, CDs-Ni(OH)2 electrode and Ni(OH)2 electrode were tested for cycling at 40 A/g for 1000 cycles. The electrochemical results are showed in Fig. 5 . From Fig. 5(a), it can be seen that the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode shows a stable charge/discharge plateau even at a high current, which shows its excellent stability. In addition, it can be seen in Fig. 5(c) that the specific capacity of the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode remained 84.6% of the initial discharge after 1000 charge/discharge cycles at 40 A/g, correspondingly, the Ni(OH)2 electrode and CDs-Ni(OH)2 electrode retention rates are only 24.3%, 45.3%, which also confirms the excellent stability of the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode. The excellent stability of the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode can be attributed to the synergistic effect between Ni and Co that can help to improve the electrochemical performance. Besides, the addition of carbon dots can not only stabilize the morphology but also increase the conductivity and thus improve the electrode performance.

To explore the enhancement of the electrical conductivity of nickel hydroxide due to the introduction of carbon dots and cobalt, EIS impedance tests were performed on the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode, CDs-Ni(OH)2 electrode and Ni(OH)2 electrode at the loading of 2.4 mg/cm2, and the result is shown in Fig. 5(b). The CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode has a smaller impedance (smaller radius of the semicircle in the first half of the curve) and a smaller diffusion impedance (higher slope of the straight line in the second half of the curve) compared to the contrast electrode. This confirms that the integrating of carbon dots and the introduction of cobalt elements make the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode have superior electrical conductivity. This observation is in consistence with the result from Fig. 4 (e-f) where the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode shows lower charge voltage plateau and higher discharge voltage plateau.

In Table 1 , the results of this work have been presented together with those reported. As can be seen from Table 1, the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode shows excellent performance in terms of both specific capacity and stability. It is significant that electrodes with good stability at high current densities seem to be more promising for applications than electrodes with a higher number of cycles but lower current densities. Thus, it can be announced that a high-performance CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode has been successfully synthesized as a material for supercapacitor, which still has the relatively excellent specific capacity, rate, and cycling performance at high current density. Especially, the capacitance retention rate can maintain 84.6% even at a current density up to 40 A/g, which is more eyecatching than many previous reports.

To further verify the excellent characteristics and wide application prospects of CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode, the Ni/Zn supercapattery was assembled with CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode as the positive electrode and metal Zn sheet as the negative electrode, the electrolyte was the mixture of 1 M KOH and 0.02 M Zn(Ac)2. The electrochemical characterization of the Ni/Zn supercapattery was performed at of 1.0–3.0 V by combining the electrochemical test results of the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode.

Fig. 6 shows the cyclic voltammetric curve and the charge/discharge curve of the Ni/Zn supercapattery at a sweep rate of 10 mV/s and a current density of 5 A/g. From Fig. 6(a), it can be seen that the CV curve has two oxidation peaks of different sizes at the potentials of 1.889 V and 2.743 V. Combined with the preparation process of the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode above, it can be determined that the smaller oxidation peak at 1.889 V is provided by the oxidation of Co(OH)2 to CoOOH, while the larger oxidation peak at 2.743 V potential is the oxidation process of Ni(OH)2 to NiOOH. It is of interest that when the reduction reaction occurs in the reverse voltage sweep, the reduction peaks corresponding to the two differently sized oxidation peaks happen to superimpose perfectly, forming a large reduction peak at a potential of 1.511 V, which corresponds to the results of the exploration of the introduction of Co content at the Ni(OH)2 electrode above. In addition, further observation of Fig. 6(b) shows that during charging, the voltage curve exhibits two charging plateaus, one long and high and one short and low, which coincide with the two oxidation peaks observed in Fig. 6(a), one large and one small; while during discharging, the voltage curve exhibits a clear and stable discharge plateau, which also corresponds to the reduction peak of only one in the CV curve. A stable discharge plateau is equivalent to a continuous and stable voltage output, which is why the Ni/Co redox reaction is superimposed as much as possible by controlling the amount of Co content in the above paper. Also, the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode exhibits a specific capacity of 242.2 mAh/g at 5 A/g current in Ni/Zn supercapattery, which reflects the excellent electrochemical properties of the CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode.

Fig. 6(c) shows the cyclic voltammetry curves of Ni/Zn supercapattery at sweep rates of 2, 5, 10, and 20 mV/s. It can be seen that when the sweep rate is gradually accelerated from small to large, the cyclic voltammetry curves show two oxidation peaks, one large and one small, and only one superimposed reduction peak, which once again confirms the stable electrochemical activity of Ni/Zn supercapattery and the success of the Co content exploration study in the previous paper. Meanwhile, the stable and consistent shape of the cyclic voltammetric curve at a high sweep rate also indicates that the Ni/Zn supercapattery still have strong stability at high current and high voltage. Fig. 6(d) and 6(e) show the test of Ni/Zn supercapattery multiplicity performance. From Fig. 6(d), it can be seen that when the current density increases from 5, 10, 20, and 40 A/g in sequence, the voltage curves all show two charging plateaus at high and low levels and a stable discharge plateau, which demonstrates the good electrochemical activity and stability of Ni/Zn supercapattery at high current. Fig. 6(e) shows the variation curve of Ni/Zn supercapattery specific capacity with current density, when the current density is 5, 10, 20, and 40 A/g, the specific capacity of Ni/Zn supercapattery are 242.2, 234.7, 219.4, and 196.7 mAh/g, respectively. From Fig. 6(f), the Ragone curve shows when the power density reaches 57.2 kW/kg, Ni/Zn supercapattery have an energy density of 281.2 Wh/kg.

Further analysis shows that when the current density increases from 5 A/g to 40 A/g, the specific capacity still retains as much as 81.3% of the original capacity and still has a stable discharge plateau, which reflects the excellent multiplicative performance of our assembled Ni/Zn supercapattery and the impressive electrochemical performance under high current rapid charge and discharge. It is worth noting that when the current density is higher, the charging cut-off voltage of the supercapattery increases, and the specific value of the charging cut-off voltage needs to be observed from the cyclic voltammetric curve. Therefore, further optimization of the electrolyte will be a feasible way to improve the performance of the supercapattery. Fig. 6(g) shows the specific capacity retention curve of the Ni/Zn supercapattery at a current density of 40 A/g for 2000 charge/discharge cycles, from which it can be seen that the specific capacity of the supercapattery is not reduced significantly after 2000 cycles compared with the initial one. This directly indicates that the Ni/Zn supercapattery cycling stability performance is already in a more desirable state. It is also worth noting that the corrosion phenomenon of the Zn negative electrode is more serious in the late cycle, and the optimization of the Zn negative electrode is also an effective idea to optimize the performance of the supercapattery.

In this paper, we successfully fabricated the Ni(OH)2 electrode containing Co and CDs by cathodic electrodeposition. Co integrating can effectively stabilize Ni(OH)2 with only 15.4% capacity loss over 1000 charge/discharge cycles at 40 A/g. Moreover, the Ni/Zn supercapattery coupled with zinc flake as the cathode and CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode as the anode exhibited excellent energy density and extraordinary cycling stability. The CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 provided a very high energy density of 281.2 Wh/kg at a power density of 57.2 kW/kg and near 100% of initial capacitance retention after 2000 cycles at 40 A/g. Such eminent performances strongly certificate that the obtained CDs-Co/Ni(OH)2 electrode is a potential Superior electrode for supercapattery application.

Yangbo Cui: Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Chong Han: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Huizheng Si: Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Kaiyu Liu: Investigation, Visualization. Hongtao Liu: Methodology, Resources. Shangbin Sang: Conceptualization, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Qiumei Wu: Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the science and technology innovation Program of Hunan Province (No. 2020RC1001), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Central South University (No. 2019zzts445), the Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Plan Project, China (No. 2017TP1001), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, 2020JJ4681) and the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2019YFA0210300).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2023.142279.
